normal: Biology is a science which is focused on studying of <mark>living organisms</mark>. (Bios – Life, logos – 'study of')
The concept of <mark>“life”</mark> is not easy to define and scientists are still unable to provide an <mark>acceptable definition</mark> for life.
“Life” is something <mark>special and unique</mark> which cannot be explained using <mark>laws of chemistry and physics</mark>.
Biology is <mark>complex and vast</mark>, therefore it is divided into three primary branches: Zoology (study of animals), Botany (study of plants), Microbiology (study of microorganisms).

Areas of study in these branches include: Cell Biology (studying cells), Histology (studying tissues), Anatomy (studying gross structure of the body), Physiology (studying functions), Biochemistry (studying biological molecules), Genetics (studying inheritance), Ecology (studying environment).
Life on earth formed around <mark>3.8 billion years ago</mark>.
The first organisms are believed to be <mark>heterotrophic, anaerobic prokaryotes</mark>.
Evolution resulted in <mark>extensive biodiversity</mark> which exists in the <mark>biosphere</mark>.
Scientists assume there are about <mark>10 to over 100 million species</mark> in the world.
Every organism has a <mark>specific role in the environment</mark> for the existence of the biosphere.

Biological diversity includes the variety of <mark>plants, animals and microorganisms</mark>, diversity of <mark>genes</mark>, and different <mark>ecosystems</mark> such as deserts, rainforests and coral reefs.
Studying <mark>histology and anatomy</mark> helps understand <mark>organ structure</mark>, functions of organ systems, and <mark>structure–function relationship</mark>.
Natural resources are <mark>limited</mark>; overuse due to <mark>population growth</mark> causes problems such as <mark>pollution, biodiversity loss, desertification</mark>.
Sustainable food production involves <mark>environmentally safe methods</mark>; methods include high yielding varieties, disease resistant varieties, and improved post-harvest technology.
Plants are <mark>primary producers</mark>; animals depend directly or indirectly on them. Understanding plants helps produce <mark>high yielding and disease resistant plants</mark>.

Knowledge of causes and effects of diseases is important for maintaining a <mark>healthy human body</mark>. Non-communicable diseases include <mark>cancer, heart disease, diabetes, chronic renal disease</mark>. Communicable diseases include <mark>dengue, AIDS</mark>.

Knowledge of biological concepts is used in <mark>legal and ethical issues</mark>, such as parentage testing, criminal investigations, and immigration disputes. <mark>DNA fingerprinting</mark> is applied.
Organisms show diversity based on <mark>size</mark>, <mark>shape</mark>, <mark>form</mark>, and <mark>habitat</mark>.

Characteristics of organisms include <mark>order and organization</mark>, <mark>metabolism</mark>, <mark>growth and development</mark>, <mark>irritability and coordination</mark>, <mark>adaptation</mark>, <mark>reproduction</mark>, and <mark>heredity and evolution</mark>.
Hierarchical levels of organization: <mark>Molecules → Organelles → Cells → Tissues → Organs → Organ systems → Organisms → Populations → Communities → Ecosystems → Biosphere</mark>.

normal: Polarity is an <mark>uneven charge distribution</mark> within a molecule.
In a water molecule, oxygen atom is slightly <mark>negative</mark> and hydrogen atom is slightly <mark>positive</mark> (δ- partial negative, δ+ partial positive).
Weak attractions between the slightly polar hydrogen atom of one water molecule and the slightly polar oxygen atom of an adjacent water molecule are known as <mark>hydrogen bonds</mark>.
Hydrogen bonds play a major role in maintaining all the <mark>properties of water</mark>.
The properties of water arise due to attractions of different water molecules. When water is in liquid form, its H bonds are <mark>very fragile</mark>, forming, breaking, and reforming frequently.
Four major properties of water to maintain life on earth: <mark>cohesive behavior, ability to moderate temperature, expansion upon freezing, versatility as a solvent</mark>.
<mark>Cohesive behavior</mark>: Attraction between water molecules due to hydrogen bonding is cohesion. Attraction between water molecules and other substances is adhesion.
Cohesion and adhesion allow water to act as a <mark>transport medium</mark>.
Due to cohesion, water and dissolved substances such as minerals and nutrients transport through vascular tissues, <mark>xylem and phloem</mark>, against gravity.
Adhesion between water molecules and cell walls also helps conduction of water and dissolved substances.
Water has a <mark>high surface tension</mark>, forming a water film on aquatic systems. Small insects (e.g., water skaters) can walk on the water surface.
<mark>Ability to moderate temperature</mark>: Water can absorb or release a relatively high amount of heat energy with slight temperature change.
High <mark>specific heat</mark> allows water to function as a <mark>thermal buffer</mark> in living systems and aquatic bodies.
High <mark>heat of vaporization</mark> allows organisms to release much heat with minimum water loss.
Examples: Evaporation of sweat maintains constant body temperature; transpiration keeps plant surfaces cool.
<mark>Expansion upon freezing</mark>: Water has maximum density at 4˚C. Below 4˚C, water forms a crystalline lattice (ice) that floats.
Ice floating is important in polar regions, allowing organisms in aquatic bodies to survive winter.

<mark>Versatility as a solvent</mark>: Water dissolves polar molecules (e.g., glucose), non-polar ionic (e.g., NaCl), and molecules with both polar and ionic regions (e.g., lysozymes) due to hydrogen bonding.
Solubility in water depends on <mark>polarity</mark>, not solely ionic nature.
revision: Polarity – <mark>uneven charge distribution</mark> in a molecule
Water molecule: Oxygen δ-, Hydrogen δ+
Hydrogen bonds – weak attractions between water molecules
Hydrogen bonds maintain <mark>properties of water</mark>
Properties of water: <mark>cohesion, moderate temperature, expansion upon freezing, solvent versatility</mark>
Cohesion – attraction between water molecules; adhesion – attraction to other substances
Water transports <mark>minerals & nutrients</mark> via <mark>xylem & phloem</mark>
High <mark>surface tension</mark> allows water film; insects can walk on water
High <mark>specific heat</mark> & <mark>heat of vaporization</mark> → thermal buffer; body/plant cooling
Expansion on freezing → maximum density at 4˚C; ice floats, protecting aquatic life
Solvent versatility: dissolves polar (glucose), ionic (NaCl), and polar-ionic molecules (lysozymes)
Solubility depends on <mark>polarity</mark>

normal: Advancement of <mark>cytology</mark> is mostly based on <mark>microscopy</mark>. The discovery and early study of cells progressed with the invention of the <mark>microscope</mark>.
Light microscope: Visible light passes through the specimen and then through glass lenses. Lenses refract light to magnify the image projected into the eye. Simplest microscope has a single lens.
Compound light microscope: Commonly used in <mark>school and medical laboratories</mark>. Resolution power and magnification are key parameters.

Magnification is the ratio of an object's image size to its actual size. Maximum magnification of a light microscope is <mark>1000×</mark>. Resolution power is the minimum distance between two points that can be distinguished (<mark>0.2 µm</mark> for light microscope).
Magnification depends on two lenses: objective and eyepiece. Total magnification = <mark>objective × eyepiece</mark>. Example: Objective = 40×, Eyepiece = 15× → Total = 600×
Electron microscope: Uses <mark>shorter wavelength electrons</mark> to overcome the resolution limit of light microscopes. Can theoretically magnify up to 1×10⁸, practically ~5×10⁵.
Electron microscopes reveal organelles and subcellular structures impossible to see with light microscopes.
Two types of electron microscopes: 1) <mark>Transmission electron microscope (TEM)</mark>, 2) <mark>Scanning electron microscope (SEM)</mark>
TEM: Studies internal structures. Electrons pass through thin, metal-stained specimens. Image reflects electron patterns.
SEM: Studies surface structure. Fine beam of electrons reflects from gold-coated specimens. Ideal for <mark>3D surface view</mark>.

Differences between Light and Electron microscopes: Light uses <mark>glass lenses</mark> & can see living objects; Electron uses <mark>magnets</mark>, requires staining with heavy metals, and observes only non-living objects.
Cell theory: All organisms are composed of cells; the cell is the <mark>basic structural and functional unit</mark>; all cells arise from <mark>pre-existing cells</mark>.

Historical contributors: Robert Hooke (1665) – coined 'cell'; Anton van Leeuwenhoek (1650) – first observed living single-celled organisms; Schleiden (1831) – all plants made of cells; Schwann (1839) – all animals made of cells; Virchow (1855) – cells arise from pre-existing cells.

Organization of cells: Two kinds – <mark>Prokaryotic</mark> and <mark>Eukaryotic</mark>. All cells share basic features: bounded by <mark>plasma membrane</mark>, contain <mark>cytosol</mark>, carry <mark>DNA</mark>, have <mark>ribosomes</mark>.

Prokaryotic vs Eukaryotic cells: Prokaryotes – <mark>bacteria, archaebacteria</mark>; Eukaryotes – <mark>protists, fungi, plants, animals</mark>. Differences include <mark>size, organelles, DNA, ribosomes, cell wall, flagella, respiration, photosynthesis</mark>.
revision: Cytology advances mainly due to <mark>microscopy</mark>
Light microscope: <mark>visible light</mark>, glass lenses, max 1000× magnification, resolution 0.2 µm
Compound microscope: used in <mark>schools and medical labs</mark>
Total magnification = objective × eyepiece (e.g., 40× × 15× = 600×)
Electron microscope: <mark>shorter wavelength electrons</mark>, reveals organelles, max practical magnification ~5×10⁵
Types: <mark>TEM</mark> – internal structures, <mark>SEM</mark> – surface 3D view
Light vs Electron: Light – glass lenses, living objects; Electron – magnets, non-living, heavy metal stains
Cell theory: all organisms composed of <mark>cells</mark>, basic unit, arise from pre-existing cells
Key contributors: <mark>Hooke, Leeuwenhoek, Schleiden, Schwann, Virchow</mark>
Cell organization: <mark>Prokaryotic vs Eukaryotic</mark>
All cells: <mark>plasma membrane, cytosol, DNA, ribosomes</mark>
Prokaryote: <mark>bacteria, archaebacteria</mark>, small, simple, no nucleus, binary fission
Eukaryote: <mark>protists, fungi, plants, animals</mark>, larger, membrane-bound organelles, mitosis/meiosis
